11.text: copy for deep-copying the base model state before LoRA injection.

47.text: The LoRA wrapper: the key innovation of this lab. Wraps an existing nn.Linear and adds two tiny matrices that learn to "steer" the original layer's output.

54.text: Takes an existing trained linear layer and wraps it with low-rank adapters.

56.text: Detach the base weight from the model. The frozen base weight holds the pre-trained knowledge.

57.text: Freeze the base weight — its requires_grad is now False. During fine-tuning, no gradients flow here.

58.text: Read the dimensions from the existing weight matrix.

59.text: lora_A: the "down-projection" (rank x d_in). Random init — this matrix learns to read important features from the input. Small random values to break symmetry.

60.text: lora_B: the "up-projection" (d_out x rank). Zero init is crucial — it makes the adapter a no-op at the start. The model behaves exactly like the base model until training begins to shift B away from zero.

62.text: Forward: base output + adapter output. Two paths, summed.

65.text: The math: W*x + B*A*x. Since B starts at zero, the adapter contributes nothing initially. As B learns non-zero values, the adapter gently steers the output. With rank=4, A is (4, d_in) and B is (d_out, 4) — tiny compared to W's (d_out, d_in).

87.text: Standard causal attention — identical to lab 03.

90.text: Q, K, V, output projections. These will be wrapped with LoRA later.

134.text: Full model — identical to lab 03 for pre-training.

166.text: Generate names from the model (used for both base and LoRA-adapted models).

189.text: Shared training step — used for both pre-training and LoRA fine-tuning.

200.text: Backprop — during LoRA fine-tuning, gradients only flow into the unfrozen LoRA parameters.

204.text: Update — only LoRA parameters move because base weights are frozen.

215.text: Create the base model for pre-training on all names.

222.text: Pre-training loop — standard next-token prediction on the full names corpus.

229.text: Generate some baseline names to see the pre-trained model's behavior.

232.text: Save the pre-trained state so we can compare with LoRA later. Deep copy because LoRA will modify the model in-place.

237.text: Choose a fine-tuning target: names starting with 'm'. LoRA will shift the distribution toward these.

238.text: Filter the dataset to only 'm' names.

251.text: Rank 4: the adapter matrices are (4 x d_in) and (d_out x 4). Tiny compared to the full weight matrices.

254.text: Recursively walk the model and wrap every nn.Linear with LoRALinear.

256.text: Find linear layers in the module tree.

257.text: Found one — replace it with a LoRA wrapper.

258.text: Swap the original linear layer for the LoRA-wrapped version in-place.

260.text: Recurse into child modules to find nested linear layers.

264.text: Only inject LoRA into the transformer layers — not embeddings or lm_head. Embedding layers don't benefit from LoRA at this scale.

265.text: Apply LoRA to each transformer layer's attention and MLP sublayers.

267.text: Recount parameters — total grows slightly because of the LoRA matrices.

268.text: Count trainable parameters — only the LoRA A and B matrices have requires_grad=True.

270.text: What percentage of parameters are trainable? Expect ~5-15% depending on architecture.

284.text: Only feed LoRA parameters to the optimizer — the frozen base weights are excluded.

285.text: Fresh optimizer for fine-tuning — only LoRA parameters.

286.text: 500 fine-tuning steps — less than pre-training because we're making a small adjustment.

288.text: Fine-tuning loop — same training step, but on 'm'-filtered data.

290.text: Train step — gradients flow through LoRA A and B only.

304.text: Generate 20 names from the LoRA-adapted model — expect a strong bias toward 'm' names.

307.text: Reload the original pre-trained weights (without LoRA) for comparison.

308.text: Restore the saved base model state.

311.text: Generate from the base model — these should show the original, unbiased distribution.

314.text: Count 'm' names from the LoRA model — should be substantially higher than base.

315.text: Count 'm' names from the base model — should reflect the natural ~4% rate.

330.text: Merge LoRA: fold the learned adapter weights back into the base weight matrix. After merging, the model is a standard nn.Linear again with zero runtime overhead.

332.text: Walk the module tree looking for LoRA-wrapped layers.

333.text: Found a LoRA layer — merge it back.

334.text: Create a fresh nn.Linear with the merged weights.

340.text: The merge: W_new = W_base + B @ A. This is mathematically identical to the LoRA forward pass but baked into a single weight matrix. No adapter overhead at inference time.

341.text: Replace the LoRA wrapper with the merged standard linear layer.

346.text: Merge all LoRA layers in the model.

352.text: Same seed — merged model should produce IDENTICAL output to the LoRA model.

355.text: Verify every name matches — merging is exact, not an approximation.

358.text: After merging, parameter count should equal the original base model — LoRA overhead is gone.

359.text: Confirm: same size as the original model, but with the fine-tuned behavior baked in.
